nativeRange.habitat: Steep outer reef slopes and walls, typically at 12–40 m depth; tends toward deeper, moderately lit zones

diet.notes: Planktivore in the wild; generally advised to offer frozen mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched frozen brine shrimp, and high-quality marine pellets multiple times daily. A refugium cultivating copepods and amphipods has been reported to improve long-term health and color.

summary: A brilliantly colored anthias from subtropical Western Pacific reef slopes, generally kept as a small harem of one male with several females; intermediate care due to high feeding frequency needs and sensitivity during acclimation.
careNotes: Pictilis Anthias are protogynous hermaphrodites — all individuals are born female, and if the dominant male is lost the largest female will often transition to male. They have been known to jump from open aquaria and a secure lid is advisable. Best kept as a harem group (one male with 2–4 females); two males in the same tank tend to result in serious aggression. Initial acclimation may be challenging: new arrivals commonly prefer lower lighting and multiple hiding spots. Feeding frequency is higher than many marine fish — three to five small feedings per day is generally advised. Sources suggest a refugium producing live copepods and amphipods can meaningfully improve long-term condition. Males display notably more vivid coloration and elongated dorsal fin filaments compared to females. In the wild this species forms aggregations above steep outer reef slopes at depths of roughly 12–40 m, suggesting it is adapted to moderate currents and somewhat dimmer light than typical shallow reef species.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been widely reported. The species is a protogynous hermaphrodite; large females will transition to male when the dominant male is removed. Breeding in aquaria would require a large, stable system with a well-established harem group.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (13.5 cm TL), temperature range (22–27°C), native range, and taxonomy. FishBase is authoritative for max length and temperature; range data from FishBase used as primary reference.

additional.name: TankFacts
additional.notes: Additional cross-check for temperature (76–80°F = 24.4–26.7°C), salinity (SG 1.023–1.026), pH (7.8–8.4), and breeding behavior. Temperature and salinity averaged across all three sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding rarely reported; protogynous hermaphroditism means sex-changing occurs naturally but spawning in captivity requires large, stable harem groups.
